coredata.dc:description: There has been significant interest in the preparation and versatile applications of carbon dots (CDs) due to their immense potential value in sensors and imaging. In this work, silicon-doped green carbon dots (Si-CDs) with high quantum yield and rich epoxypropyl were effectively synthesized. Given the clinical diagnostic importance of abnormal levels of tyrosinase (TYR), sensitive detection of TYR is significant for clinical research. A fluorescence signal-off strategy with Si-CDs as probe was constructed to determine TYR based on the oxidation of dopamine by TYR. The detection ranges of this method were 0.01–1.5 and 10–30 U/mL with the detection limit of 0.0046 U/mL, the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 0.01 U/mL, and TYR was successfully and accurately monitored in human serum. Additionally, due to the role of lysosomes in cellular regulatory processes, including TYR levels and fluorescence stability characteristics of Si-CDs in acidic conditions, it was envisaged to use Si-CDs as probe to establish real-time monitoring of lysosomes. According to fluorescence colocation analysis, Si-CDs had intrinsic lysosomal targeting ability to HepG2 and L-02 (with Pearson correlation coefficients were 0.90 and 0.91, respectively). The targeting of Si-CDs to lysosomes was due to the acidophilic effect of the epoxypropyl on its surface.

head.ce:title: Effectively synthesized functional Si-doped carbon dots with the applications in tyrosinase detection and lysosomal imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: There has been significant interest in the preparation and versatile applications of carbon dots (CDs) due to their immense potential value in sensors and imaging. In this work, silicon-doped green carbon dots (Si-CDs) with high quantum yield and rich epoxypropyl were effectively synthesized. Given the clinical diagnostic importance of abnormal levels of tyrosinase (TYR), sensitive detection of TYR is significant for clinical research. A fluorescence signal-off strategy with Si-CDs as probe was constructed to determine TYR based on the oxidation of dopamine by TYR. The detection ranges of this method were 0.01–1.5 and 10–30 U/mL with the detection limit of 0.0046 U/mL, the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 0.01 U/mL, and TYR was successfully and accurately monitored in human serum. Additionally, due to the role of lysosomes in cellular regulatory processes, including TYR levels and fluorescence stability characteristics of Si-CDs in acidic conditions, it was envisaged to use Si-CDs as probe to establish real-time monitoring of lysosomes. According to fluorescence colocation analysis, Si-CDs had intrinsic lysosomal targeting ability to HepG2 and L-02 (with Pearson correlation coefficients were 0.90 and 0.91, respectively). The targeting of Si-CDs to lysosomes was due to the acidophilic effect of the epoxypropyl on its surface.

In this work, a fluorescence strategy was developed for tyrosinase (TYR) detection using Si-CDs as probe and dopamine as substrate based on the static quenching effect (SQE) and internal filtration effect (IFE) mechanism (Scheme 1a). In addition, the acidophilic effect of the epoxypropyl group on the surface of Si-CDs enabled them to accumulate in acidic lysosomes for targeted imaging of lysosomes (Scheme 1b).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional photoluminescent carbon-based nanomaterials with a typical size of <10 nm. CDs have attracted tremendous attention due to their unique properties, such as tunable photoluminescence, favorable photostability, facile preparation, and controllable surface functional modification [1–3]. Due to the above-mentioned outstanding advantages, CDs have been extensively used in biosensing [4], bioimaging [5,6], photo/electrocatalysis [7], drug delivery [8], and disease diagnosis [9]. Several methods, including laser ablation [10], electrochemical oxidation [11], hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment [12], melting method [13], and microwave passivation [14], have been developed to prepare CDs. With the in-depth study of CDs, the bare CDs are limited in meeting clinical application requirements. People are looking for functional CDs with specific photophysical and chemical properties that are favorable for biomedical applications. The size of the carbon core, defect/edge, functional groups, and heteroatom doping are significant factors influencing the performance of CDs, like the fluorescence ability and targeting [15–17]. Correspondingly, effectively functional CDs with specific ideal properties can be developed by selecting carbon precursors with specific properties [18], modulating pH [19], regulating carbonization temperature and time [20], surface functionalization [21] and heteroatom doping [22]. Surface-modified CDs through conjugation or doping can enhance and modulate the photophysical and chemical properties of CDs for variable applications [5,23,24]. However, there are limited reports on the dual application of target-specific analysis and localization imaging through specific structural design or surface functionalization modification of CDs. It is significant to reasonably design functional CDs based on their photophysical and chemical tunability directed by specific targets and further extend the application fields of CDs.

Tyrosinase (TYR) is a copper-containing monooxygenase that catalyzes the oxidation of phenolic substrates to o-quinones, which triggers melanin formation [25]. Melanin plays a crucial role in controlling skin color, protecting skin cells, and eliminating reactive oxygen species (ROS) [26]. Abnormal TYR levels are closely associated with Parkinson's syndrome, vitiligo, and melanoma [27], indicating significant clinical diagnosis value [28]. Hitherto, various analytical methods have been reported for TYR detection, such as electrochemistry [29], high-performance liquid chromatography [30], electrophoresis [31], and colorimetry [32]. However, these methods are typically time-consuming and require complicated operational procedures, and specialized instruments [33]. Compared to these methods, fluorescent sensing technology has attracted considerable interest in biosensors due to its low cost, real-time performance, and controllable sample pretreatment [34]. Although several fluorescence probes for TYR determination have been developed [26,33,35], these probes suffer from cumbersome synthesis processes, poor stability, and cytotoxicity, making them fewer ideal candidates. Unfortunately, most of the fluorescent CDs for TYR assay have low quantum yields (QY) [36] and blue emission centers [28,37,38], which hinder their biological applications. Therefore, it is essential to pursue CDs with enhanced fluorescence QY and red-shift emission wavelength for the determination of TYR to improve their application in biosensors and biomedicine.

Lysosomes act as the waste disposal system of the cell and are the key regulators of various cellular processes, including autophagy, cell signal transduction, and cell death [39]. Several investigations have demonstrated that lysosomal dysfunction results in severe diseases, such as neurodegenerative diseases, glucose metabolism dysfunction, and cardiovascular diseases [18,40]. Therefore, real-time and stronger evidence through monitoring damages or abnormalities in lysosomes will be supplied for the early diagnosis and treatment of these diseases. Efficient tools for the visualization of lysosomes are essential. In general, two types of lysosomotropic reagents containing acidophilic dyes (morpholine, neutral red, acridine orange) and some large molecules (Alexa 594 bound glucan and BODIPY bound bovine serum albumin) are used for lysosomal imaging [41]. However, the specificity and photostability of the probes need improvement for enhanced performance of cellular imaging. Excitingly, specially prepared CDs with superior permeability are anticipated to be favorable candidate probes for particular bioimaging [42,43]. In recent years, a variety of functionalized CDs have been designed for cell imaging, particularly for organelle imaging [44–46]. While the accomplishment of clear surface functionalization of CDs with a clarified targeting mechanism for lysosomal imaging is still lacking [47].

The doping of silicon (as an important biological element) into carbon dots (CDs) offers several advantages, including modulation of their electronic properties, creation of additional active sites (improving interaction with the target), enhancing the biomarker ability, and expanding their applications in biosensors and imaging [5,48]. In this study, silicon-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) were synthesized using phthalic acid and triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane as precursors through the solvothermal method. The resulting Si-CDs had red-shift emission, high fluorescence quantum yield (QY Si-CDs = 68.83%), and were rich in carboxyl, hydroxy and acidophilic functional groups of epoxypropyl on the surface. A fluorescence strategy was then developed for tyrosinase (TYR) detection using Si-CDs as probe and dopamine as substrate based on the static quenching effect (SQE) and internal filtration effect (IFE) mechanism. This method exhibited excellent analytical performance for determining TYR (Scheme 1 a). In addition, the acidophilic effect of the epoxypropyl group on the surface of Si-CDs enabled them to accumulate in acidic lysosomes for targeted imaging of lysosomes (Scheme 1b). This study demonstrated that the design and development of functional CDs with specific properties, directed by the physical and chemical properties of the target. Moreover, the designed Si-CDs were a typical example of achieving precise detection or localization of closely related targets since the increase in lysosomal proteinase level is associated with the reduction in tyrosinase protein level [49,50].

Tyrosinase (TYR) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dopamine (DA), triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane, phthalic acid, rhodamine 6G, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazo-lium bromide (MTT), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), trypsin (TRY), bovine albumin (BSA), isoprenaline (ISO) and hyaluronic acid (HA) were supplied by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH) and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. ER-Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red, Mito-Tracker Red, Golgi-Tracker Red and Hoechst-33342 were purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology. All reagents do not require further processing.

Fluorescence and UV–visible absorption spectrum/intensity were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, USA) and a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan), respectively. An FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain) was used to record the fluorescence lifetime decay curves. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (NICOLET iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were employed to analyze the morphology and molecular structure of Si-CDs. Fluorescence microscopy images were collected using a confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, TCS SP8, GER). The cell viability was measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Si-CDs were prepared using a one-pot solvothermal method. Initially, 0.4984 g (150 mM) phthalic acid and 0.8353 g (150 mM) of triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane were sonicated in 20 mL of methanol and then transferred to 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor for the reaction to take place at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane and purified with a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 0–500 Da for 48 h. And approximately 0.5670 g of Si-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying. Finally, a stock solution of Si-CDs (6.3 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving it in water and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of relative fluorescence quantum yield of Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative fluorescence quantum yield of Si-CDs (QY Si-CDs) was determined with rhodamine 6G as a reference. Firstly, the absorptions of Si-CDs (aqueous solution) and rhodamine 6G (ethanol solution, QY R hodamine 6G = 95%) at 430 nm were measured (A<0.06). Then, the fluorescence spectra of the above solutions were measured at 430 nm excitation. The curve slope ratio of Si-CDs to rhodamine 6G (

) was obtained by taking absorbance as the X axis and the integral area of fluorescence spectrum as the Y axis, and QY Si-CDs was calculated according to Formula 1.

To determine the TYR, a mixture of 63 μg/mL Si-CDs and 1 mM DA in 200 μL pH 6.6 PBS was mixed with different concentrations of TYR standard solutions (0.01, 0.05, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 1.0, 1.5, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 14, 16, 18, 20, 25, 30, 40, 60 U/mL) and reacted for 150 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 440–600 nm range with 430 nm excitation, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE, (F0–F)/F0) was further calculated. The mixture of Si-CDs with DA or TYR was defined as Si-CDs + DA or Si-CDs + TYR, respectively. The solution of Si-CDs + DA for TYR detection was defined as Si-CDs + DA + TYR. The F and F0 were the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs in Si-CDs + DA + TYR with and without TYR, respectively.

The effect of common interferents, including TYR, HRP, CAT, SOD, TRY, BSA, ISO, Glu, HA, GSH, and AA, was evaluated on the responses of Si-CDs + DA and Si-CDs + DA + TYR, respectively. Specifical, each interferent was added to a solution of 63 μg/mL Si-CDs and 1 mM DA in pH 6.6 PBS in the absence or presence of 10 U/mL TYR, and the reaction was controlled at 37 °C for 150 min.

The recovery of TYR was tested in human serum, which was obtained from the blood through centrifugation. Then, the assay for the recovery was conducted in a solution of 200 μL 1% serum diluted by pH 6.6 PBS. Different concentrations of TYR (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 1.0 U/mL) were added to the mixed solution of 63 μg/mL Si-CDs and 1 mM DA respectively, followed by a 150 min reaction at 37 °C. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 440–600 nm with 430 nm excitation.

HepG2 (hepatoma cell) and L-02 (hepatocyte cell) were seeded into confocal culture dishes (1 mL, 5 × 103 cell/well) and incubated for 24 h. The medium was then removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS. Next, 1 mL of DMEM and 1640 medium containing 100 μg/mL of Si-CDs were added and incubated for another 24 h, respectively. Afterward, the cells were pre-treated with different organelle dyes (ER-Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red, Mito-Tracker Red, Golgi-Tracker Red, and Hoechst-33342) for 40 min, respectively. The images were obtained by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) under excitation wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm, and 552 nm. Finally, the data were processed using Image J.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and structural characterization of Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the optical properties, morphology and molecular structure of Si-CDs were characterized. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the Si-CDs exhibited green luminescence under UV lamp irradiation (inset in Fig. 1A) with optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 430 nm and 506 nm, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Si-CDs displayed characteristic absorption peaks at 279 nm and the broad absorption centered at 416 nm, which were ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bond (sp2 hybridization) and the n-π* transition of CO bond (sp2 hybridization), respectively [51]. The presence of the CO bond might introduce new energy levels inside Si-CDs, which affected their fluorescence emission [52]. Si-CDs also showed excitation-independent luminescence behavior at the variable excitation wavelengths (300–480 nm) (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence QY is the important parameter for measuring optical property, and the relative QY of Si-CDs was calculated to be 68.83% with rhodamine 6G as a reference (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, the production yield (PY) of Si-CDs was approximately 42.5% based on the raw materials of 0.4984 g phthalic acid and 0.8353 g triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane) [53]. The morphology of Si-CDs was characterized by TEM, as displayed in Fig. 1D, and showed regular spheroids with a relatively uniform distribution, with particle sizes ranging from 1.27 nm to 4.19 nm and an average diameter of 2.35 nm. The amorphous property of Si-CDs precluded the observation of their lattice structure. Chemical composition and functional groups in Si-CDs were further investigated (Fig. 1E–I). The FTIR spectra of phthalic acid, triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane, and Si-CDs were shown in (Fig. 1E). The phthalic acid displayed a wide characteristic absorption band at 3400-2500 cm−1 for stretching vibrations of O–H and C–H. The peaks at 1582 cm−1 and 1496 cm−1 belonged to stretching vibrations of CC on aromatic rings [54]. The stretching vibrations of CO and C–O on carboxyl functional groups were located at 1685 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1, respectively. The bending vibration of C–H was noticed at 1275 cm−1. The FTIR spectra of triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane exhibited C–H, C–O and Si–O stretching vibrations peak at 2932 cm−1, 1394 cm−1, and 1106 cm−1. The characteristic absorption peak at 909 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of -CH(O)CH- [5]. Meanwhile, the FTIR of Si-CDs showed strong absorption at 3395 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H, while the absorption peaks at 1730 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, and 1105 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, C–O, and Si–O, respectively [5]. This indicated that Si-CDs had been successfully doped with silicon element. The characteristic absorption peak at 914 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of –CH(O)CH-, which indicated that Si-CDs were rich in the eosinophilic functional group (–CH(O)CH–) on the surface. Moreover, the XPS spectrum of Si-CDs (Fig. 1F) displayed four typical peaks at 102.12 eV, 153.08 eV, 284.78 eV and 532.24 eV attributed to Si2p, Si2s, C1s and O1s, respectively. The corresponding atomic contents were 5.71% (Si), 66.59% (C) and 27.70% (O). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) could be divided into three parts at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC of graphene carbon, C–O of sp3 hybrid carbon, and CO of carboxyl carbon [55,56]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1H) exhibited the distinctive peaks of CO, Si–O, and C–O at 531.2 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.0 eV, respectively [57]. The high-resolution XPS of Si2p spectrum (Fig. 1I) illustrated two component peaks at 101.8 eV (Si–C) and 102.4 eV (Si–O), respectively [58]. The FTIR and XPS analyses revealed that Si-CDs were doped with Si element and the surface was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups such as O–H, –COOH, Si–O and –CH(O)CH-. The successfully doped Si element (with lower electronegativity than C) and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface (such as CO, –CH(O)CH-) might have significant effects on the modulation of electronic distribution, optical properties (green luminescence, high fluorescence QY), and physicochemical properties (water-soluble, acidophilic) of Si-CDs [15].

The fluorescence stability of Si-CDs was evaluated under various conditions. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs remained stable between 5 and 55 °C, whereas a slight decrease was observed when the temperature increased gradually from 75 to 95 °C (Fig. S1A), indicating that high temperature affected the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs to some extent. Since the temperature ranges in biological systems are usually lower than 40 °C, the impact of temperature on Si-CDs for biological applications can be disregarded. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs remained unchanged at pH 3.0–7.0, and marginally decreased in an alkaline environment (pH 8.0–12.0), indicating that Si-CDs were relatively stable in neutral and even enhanced in acidic environments (Fig. S1B). The unique but vulnerable feature of Si-CDs under weakly acidic conditions could lead to special applications. Meanwhile, Si-CDs were almost unaffected in high saline solutions, indicating their stability in the presence of salt ions (Fig. S1C). Si-CDs also exhibited photobleaching resistance as their fluorescence intensities were maintained above 95% after continuous excitation at 430 nm for 1 h (Fig. S1D). Finally, as shown in Fig. S1E, the Si-CDs showed low fluorescence reactivity towards common metal ions (Pb2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+). This may be due to the weak coordination between these metal ions and Si-CDs, which hindered the non-radiative electron transition and stabilized the fluorescence of Si-CDs [59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detecting feasibility and mechanism of TYR using Si-CDs + DA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 , it was observed that Si-CDs were stable in the presence of DA or TYR. While, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs changed significantly with the addition of TYR to the mixture of Si-CDs and DA. Additionally, as the concentration of TYR increased from 6.25 U/mL to 12.5 U/mL, the fluorescence QE of Si-CDs increased from 35.49% to 54.37%. Thus, it was concluded that the fluorescence signal-off strategy, which involved DA as the substrate and Si-CDs as the probe, could be employed to determine the activity of TYR.

The quenching mechanism of Si-CDs by introducing TYR was investigated, as shown in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3A, it can be seen that the fluorescence lifetimes of Si-CDs, Si-CDs + DA, Si-CDs + TYR, and Si-CDs + DA + TYR were 8.67 ns, 8.29 ns, 8.66 ns, and 8.69 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of Si-CDs almost did not change with the addition of DA, TYR, and DA + TYR, indicating the absence of a dynamic quenching effect (DQE) of Si-CDs in Si-CDs + DA + TYR. Compared with DA or TYR, the absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B) of DA + TYR (the reaction solution of DA and TYR) exhibited a new shoulder peak (307 nm) and a broad absorption peak centered at 460 nm, both of which increased with the increase of TYR (inset in Fig. 3B), indicating that TYR catalyzed the oxidation of DA to generate the new electron-deficient oxidation product of dopaquinone, serving as an excellent fluorescence quencher [38,61]. The molecular structures of DA, DA + TYR and Si-CDs + DA + TYR were evaluated by FTIR (Fig. 3C). The absorption peaks of DA at 3120 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching and bending vibrations of O–H respectively, while the absorption peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1510 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC on the aromatic ring, and the absorption peak at 957 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of CH2–NH2. Among them, the absorption peaks at 1190 cm−1 and 957 cm−1 were the characteristic absorption peaks of DA [62]. Compared with DA, the main absorption peaks of DA + TYR at 3440 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and CC on aromatic rings, respectively. At the same time, the absorption peak near 1600 cm−1 was strong and wide, indicating the partial oxidation of phenolic hydroxides [63]. The absorption peaks at 1190 cm−1 and 957 cm−1 disappeared, indicating that DA was oxidized by TYR. The terminal amino group of dopaquinone instead of DA might interact with -COOH or O–H on the surface of Si-CDs to form amide bonds or intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The FTIR of the reacting system showed that the peak positions of Si-CDs + DA + TYR and DA + TYR remained almost identical (Fig. 3C). However, the peak intensities of Si-CDs + DA + TYR at 1400 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 were significantly stronger than that of DA + TYR, and these two peaks were attributed to the stretching vibration of C–N and CO respectively, indicating the presence of an amide bond between Si-CDs and DA + TYR. The newly formed amide bond between the fluorophore (Si-CDs) and the quenching agent (dopaquinone, produced by the oxidation of DA caused by TYR in the DA + TYR system) suggested the happening of SQE based on the stable non-fluorescent complex in the signal-off system. Furthermore, according to the Stern-Volmer curve (Fig. 3D) [64] and Formula S1 [65], the quenching constant (K) of Si-CDs decreased from 0.24506 to 0.22034 with the temperatures rising from 37 °C to 45 °C, suggesting the increased degree of the disorder of the quenching system with temperature [66]. Thus, it was confirmed the presence of SQE between Si-CDs and DA + TYR was ascribed to the formed non-fluorescent complex [28]. Additionally, the broad absorption peak centered at 460 nm of dopaquinone produced by the oxidation of DA by TYR (the blue-green curve in Fig. 3E) coincided with the fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs (the red and yellow curves in Fig. 3E), suggesting a possible IFE between Si-CDs and DA + TYR. The QE correction was performed according to the parameters of the quartz cuvette geometry (inset in Fig. 3F) and Formula S2 [67]. As shown in Fig. 3F and Table S1, the detected and corrected QE gradually deviated with the increase in TYR concentrations. Moreover, the total fluorescence inhibition of Si-CDs + DA by TYR through IFE was up to 22.15% (SE, calculated according to Formula S3). The results suggested that there was a partial IFE of DA + TYR in the quenching process of Si-CDs. In summary, the TYR detection strategy with Si-CDs as the probe and DA as the substrate was based on the SQE and IFE between Si-CDs and DA + TYR, which sensitively regulated the fluorescence signal of Si-CDs due to the existence of amide bond, intermolecular hydrogen bond and spectral overlap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions of TYR detection
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the enzyme-catalyzed reaction between DA and TYR was investigated, and the effects of various parameters such as reaction time, temperature, pH and DA concentration on the detection system were investigated. It was observed that with the extension of the reaction time from 15 min to 180 min, the effect of DA on the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs was negligible, While the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs + DA + TYR gradually decreased (insert in Fig. S2A), and the QE of TYR on Si-CDs + DA gradually increased (Fig. S2A), suggesting an increase in dopaquinone production due to the TYR-catalyzed oxidation of DA, and further modulated the fluorescence of Si-CDs to a greater extent. Considering that the reaction time was too long and vulnerable to external influences, the reaction time shall be properly controlled. As the majority of enzymatic catalytic reactions occur under mild conditions, the QE of DA + TYR for Si-CDs increased and then decreased with the increasing reaction temperature (25–60 °C), reaching the maximum at 37 °C (Fig. S2B). However, low or high temperatures might affect the enzyme catalytic activity of TYR. Therefore, 150 min and 37 °C were selected as the optimal reaction time and temperature for the TYR assay. Moreover, it was observed that alkaline conditions could lead to self-polymerization of DA and affect the fluorescence stability of the probe [51]. Therefore, the effect of a weak acid environment on the fluorescence signal of Si-CDs + DA + TYR was investigated. As the pH increased from 5.0 to 6.6, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs + DA + TYR decreased continuously (inset in Fig. S2C), and the QE of DA + TYR on Si-CDs increased gradually (Fig. S2C). Therefore, 6.6 was selected as the reaction pH. On the other hand, as the substrate, the concentration of DA had a great influence on the reaction rate. As shown in Fig. S2D, with the increase of DA concentrations, the QE of DA + TYR on Si-CDs increased until reaching the platform. Thus, 1 mM DA was chosen as the optimal substrate concentration for the TYR detection system.

Under the optimized conditions, the effect of TYR concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs + DA was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of TYR, the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs + DA gradually decreased. The QE ((F0–F)/F0) gradually enhanced and reached the plateau (Fig. 4B). This plateau occurred because the fixed concentration of DA saturated the enzyme-catalyzed reaction with the rise of TYR concentrations. Moreover, the QE of Si-CDs showed a good linear relationship with the concentration of TYR (CTYR) in the range of 0.01–1.5 U/mL and 10–30 U/mL respectively, and the fitted linear equations were Y = 0.17778CTYR+0.02243 (R2 = 0.99358) (I) and Y = 0.00853CTYR+0.58266 (R2 = 0.99423) (II), respectively ((I) (II) inset in Fig. 4B). Based on the 3σ/S (where σ represents the standard deviation of the blank and S is the slope of the linear fitting equation, n = 11), the LOD was calculated to be 0.0046 U/mL. Usually, the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ), acquired from the lowest point of the standard curve, is the lowest concentration of analyte in the sample that can be reliably quantified. The LLOQ of TYR was 0.01 U/mL with a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 11) of 4.35%. Actually, the presence of interfering substances might have negative impacts on the detection of TYR. Therefore, the effects of common interfering substances (TYR, HRP, CAT, SOD, TRY, BSA, ISO, Glu, HA, GSH and AA) on the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs + DA and Si-CDs + DA + TYR were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, TYR could selectively catalyze the oxidation of DA and quench the fluorescence signal of Si-CDs, except that HRP had a slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs + DA. Meanwhile, the QE of Si-CDs + DA + TYR remained unchanged when these common interfering substances were added to Si-CDs + DA + TYR alone or completely, indicating that the signal-off strategy based on Si-CDs + DA had high selectivity for TYR detection. Then, this method was evaluated for the determination of TYR in normal human serum, as shown in Table S2. The spiked recovery of TYR was 91.53%–110.6%, and the RSD was 0.10%–5.57%. The fluctuation in recovery and RSDs among different serum samples may be due to the differences in serum components among the serum contributors. It demonstrated that this method had good accuracy in the determination of TYR and was practical for use in the analysis. In comparison to existing methods (Table S3), this study established a fluorescence method with Si-CDs as probe and DA as a substrate to detect TYR with high sensitivity and wide linear range through a controllable mixing procedure. In addition, this method had the advantages of simple operation and not requiring expensive instruments, making it suitable for facile analysis of TYR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cytotoxicity and lysosomal targeting imaging of Si-CDs to cells
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the potential biological application, the cytotoxicity of Si-CDs on cells was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S3, the cell survival rates of HepG2 and L-02 exceeded 87.9% and 97.1% when co-cultured with different concentrations of Si-CDs (12.5–400 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively. It showed that Si-CDs had good biocompatibility with HepG2 and L-02. At a concentration of 400 μg/mL, Si-CDs showed a slight killing effect on HepG2 but had a slight growth-promoting effect on L-02. Furthermore, HepG2 and L-02 were selected as the research objects, and the commercial organelle dyes (ER-Tracker Red, Golgi-Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red, Mito-Tracker Red, and Hoechst-33342) were used as a reference, and then the fluorescent colocalization analysis of Si-CDs and organelle dyes were conducted to investigate which organelle Si-CDs tended to accumulate in. Fig. 5 showed the fluorescence co-localization images of Si-CDs with commercial Lyso-Tracker Red dyes. It was found that the fluorescent signal of Si-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Red in HepG2 and L-02 were highly overlapped (Fig. 5 D/J), and the change trends of green and red fluorescence marked by solid yellow lines were highly similar (Fig. 5 F/L). Accordingly, the Pearson correlation coefficients (P) were 0.90 and 0.91 (Fig. 5 E/K), respectively. In contrast, the P values of Si-CDs and other organelle dyes such as ER-Tracker, Golgi-Tracker, Mito-Tracker and Hoechst-33342 were lower (PER = 0.79/0.76 (Fig. S4 E/K), PGolgi = 0.80/0.84 (Fig. S5 E/K), PMito = 0.48/0.52 (Fig. S6 E/K), and PHoechst-33342 = 0.21/0.74 (Fig. S7 E/K)). It showed that the Si-CDs had good lysosomal targeting ability. Given that lysosomes were in the acidic range, and Si-CDs had fluorescence stability in acidic environments, it was envisaged that Si-CDs could be used as a potential fluorescence indicator for lysosome imaging. Si-CDs were prepared from triethoxy(3-glycidyloxypropyl)silane and possessed a rich eosinophilic functional group (–CH(O)CH–) on the surface, which could promote the targeting of Si-CDs to lysosomes based on the protonation of –CH(O)CH- by acid catalysis from the large amount of H+ in lysosomes. The protonation of –CH(O)CH- induced the opening of the ring and the generation of carbonium ions and C–OH [68,69]. The increase of C–OH promoted the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds between C–OH and –COOH/-OH on the surface of Si-CDs. The internal conjugation length of Si-CDs increased by the hydrogen bonds, stabilized the fluorescence emission of Si-CDs [70,71], which corresponded to the fluorescence intensities of Si-CDs at pH 3.0–7.0 being higher than pH 8.0–12.0 (Fig. S1B). Therefore, the –CH(O)CH- functional group present on the surface of Si-CDs exhibited a similar acidophilic effect as that of the confirmed probes for amine-targeted lysosomes. This effect enabled Si-CDs to accumulate more efficiently in acidic lysosomes [43,47], making them an attractive tool for tracking and monitoring the status of lysosomal with their lysosomal targeting imaging properties.

In this study, we utilized a one-step solvothermal treatment to synthesize green-emitting Si-CDs with exceptional luminescent properties, high biocompatibility, and lysosomal targeting ability. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (such as CO, –CH(O)CH-) and the doping of Si may play a crucial role in modulating the electronic distribution, optical and physicochemical properties of Si-CDs. Accordingly, based on the amide bond, hydrogen bond and absorption characteristics, the electron transfer between Si-CDs and dopaquinone was promoted, and the fluorescence sensing strategy for TYR determination was successfully constructed. Additionally, Si-CDs exhibited intrinsic lysosomal targeting ability to HepG2 and L-02, indicating its potential as an excellent candidate indicator for lysosomal commercial dyes. This research provided new insight into developing and applying functional CDs with specific properties based on the physical and chemical properties of the targets.
